title: Designing Magnetic Topological Insulator Trilayers for Highly-Efficient Spin-Orbit Torque Switching
authors: Ling-Jie Zhou, Deyi Zhuo, Han Tay, Zi-Jie Yan, Pu Xiao, Xiaoda Liu, Bomin Zhang, Cui-Zu Chang
abstract: Spin-orbit torque (SOT) enables efficient electrical control of magnetization, offering a pathway towards low-power spintronic devices. Magnetic topological insulators (TIs), with spin-momentum-locked surface states and intrinsic ferromagnetism, provide a unique platform for realizing SOT switching of edge current chirality in quantum anomalous Hall (QAH) insulators. In this work, we employ molecular beam epitaxy to synthesize a series of magnetic TI trilayers with controlled layer thicknesses on heat-treated SrTiO3(111) substrates. Electrical transport measurements reveal that SOT-driven magnetization reversal and the associated switching of edge current chirality are governed by the SrTiO3(111) substrate-induced charging effect, which generates an asymmetric chemical-potential alignment between the top and bottom magnetic TI layers. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the switching polarity and efficiency can be tuned through heterostructure design, gate voltage, and in-plane magnetic field, consistent with SOT symmetry. These findings identify chemical potential asymmetry as the origin of the large SOT switching ratio in magnetic TI trilayers and establish a route for electrical control of edge current chirality in QAH insulators. This work advances the understanding of SOT switching mechanism in magnetic topological materials and paves the way for next-generation, energy-efficient QAH-based logic and memory devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates how to achieve efficient magnetization switching in magnetic topological insulator trilayers by leveraging substrate-induced chemical potential asymmetry. By controlling the heterostructure design and gating, the researchers show that the switching of edge current chirality in the Quantum Anomalous Hall state can be effectively manipulated. This work provides a pathway for developing energy-efficient spintronic logic and memory devices.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind Spin-Orbit Torque (SOT)-driven magnetization switching and the associated switching of edge current chirality in magnetic topological insulator trilayers.
main_result: The researchers identified that a substrate-induced charging effect from SrTiO3 creates an asymmetric chemical potential between the top and bottom layers, preventing SOT cancellation and enabling efficient magnetization reversal.
method: Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) was used to synthesize magnetic TI trilayers, followed by electrical transport measurements using DC current pulses and gate voltage tuning in a Physical Property Measurement System (PPMS).
model_or_system: Magnetic topological insulator (mTI) trilayers consisting of Cr-doped (Bi,Sb)2Te3 layers separated by a 4 quintuple layer (QL) (Bi,Sb)2Te3 spacer, grown on heat-treated SrTiO3(111) substrates.
key_observables: Hall resistance (rho_yx), longitudinal resistance (rho_xx), Hall angle, and the normalized switching ratio (delta_rho_yx/rho_yx(0)).
important_parameters: Layer thicknesses (m, n), gate voltage (Vg), in-plane magnetic field (mu_0Hx), and chemical potential (Ef) asymmetry.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the trilayer/bilayer structure and transport results (rho_xx, rho_yx) vs magnetic field and gate voltage; Fig 2 illustrates the SOT switching process and the dependence of the switching ratio on pulse current.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the SOT switching problem in magnetic TIs, describes the MBE growth and device fabrication, presents transport measurements characterizing the QAH state, details the SOT switching experiments, and concludes with a mechanistic explanation based on substrate-induced chemical potential asymmetry.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
